name: Rod Blagojevich
charges: Wire fraud (10 counts), soliciting bribes, attempting to sell U.S. Senate seat vacated by Barack Obama
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Northern District of Illinois (Federal)
outcome: Sentenced to 14 years in federal prison. Trump commuted sentence on February 18, 2020 (served ~8 years). Fully pardoned by Trump on February 10, 2025.
source1_description: NPR - Trump Commutes Blagojevich Sentence (reports conviction details)
source2_description: MPR News - Trump pardons ex-Illinois Gov. Rod Blagojevich
notes: First jury in 2010 convicted on 1 count (lying to FBI); 2011 retrial convicted on 17 counts including 10 wire fraud counts and soliciting bribes. Sentence commuted February 2020; fully pardoned February 2025.

name: Bob McDonnell
charges: Honest services fraud, extortion, and conspiracy (11 felony counts) related to accepting $175,000 in gifts and loans from a businessman
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Eastern District of Virginia (Federal)
outcome: Originally convicted on 11 counts and sentenced to 2 years in prison (January 6, 2015). Never served time — U.S. Supreme Court unanimously vacated convictions on June 27, 2016. DOJ dismissed the case in September 2016.
source1_description: SCOTUSblog - McDonnell v. United States (conviction vacated)
source2_description: Politico - Supreme Court overturns Bob McDonnell's corruption convictions
notes: Conviction unanimously vacated 8-0 by the U.S. Supreme Court in McDonnell v. United States (2016). DOJ subsequently dropped all charges. Never served prison time.

name: George Ryan
charges: Racketeering, mail fraud, making false statements to the FBI, tax fraud, and related charges (22 counts)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Northern District of Illinois (Federal)
outcome: Convicted on all counts on April 17, 2006. Sentenced to 6.5 years (78 months) in federal prison on September 6, 2006. Served more than 5 years before release; also served 7 months of home confinement.
source1_description: DOT Inspector General - Operation Safe Road: Former Illinois Governor Sentenced to 6 Years
source2_description: WTTW News - Former Illinois Gov. George Ryan, imprisoned for corruption, dies
notes: Conviction stemmed largely from corruption as Illinois Secretary of State (1991–1999) including illegal CDL license scheme tied to six children's deaths, and continued corruption as Governor. Died in 2025.

name: Kwame Kilpatrick
charges: Racketeering, extortion, mail fraud, wire fraud, tax evasion, and related charges (24 counts)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Eastern District of Michigan (Federal)
outcome: Convicted by jury on 24 counts on March 11, 2013. Sentenced to 28 years in federal prison on October 10, 2013. Trump commuted sentence in January 2021; released from prison approximately 20 years early. Conviction remains.
source1_description: DOJ USAO-EDMI - Former Detroit Mayor Kwame Kilpatrick Sentenced to 28 Years
source2_description: WDET 101.9 FM - Kilpatrick's divisive legacy lingers in Detroit 10 years after conviction
notes: Ran city of Detroit like a criminal enterprise per prosecutors. Trump commuted sentence in 2021 but conviction stands. Cannot run for Michigan office until 2033 under state law. Over $4.8 million in restitution outstanding.

name: Ray Nagin
charges: Conspiracy, bribery, honest services wire fraud, money laundering, and tax violations (20 of 21 counts)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Eastern District of Louisiana (Federal)
outcome: Convicted by jury on 20 of 21 counts on February 12, 2014. Sentenced to 10 years in federal prison on July 9, 2014 with $585,000+ in restitution/forfeiture. Released to house arrest; probation completed March 2024.
source1_description: DOJ USAO-EDLA - Former New Orleans Mayor C. Ray Nagin Sentenced to 10 Years
source2_description: Ray Nagin Wikipedia - Conviction and sentencing details
notes: First New Orleans mayor ever criminally charged for corruption in office. Accepted more than $500,000 in payoffs from businessmen in exchange for city contracts. Mayor during Hurricane Katrina (2005). Completed supervised probation March 2024.

name: Randy 'Duke' Cunningham
charges: Conspiracy to commit bribery, honest services fraud, and tax evasion (2-count Information); accepted $2.4 million in bribes from defense contractors
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of California (Federal)
outcome: Pleaded guilty November 28, 2005. Sentenced to 100 months (8 years, 4 months) in prison and $1.8 million in restitution on March 3, 2006. Released from home confinement June 4, 2013. Granted conditional pardon by Trump on January 13, 2021.
source1_description: DOJ Press Release - Former Congressman Cunningham Sentenced to More Than 8 Years
source2_description: NPR - Former Rep. 'Duke' Cunningham Freed After Bribery Sentence
notes: Longest prison term ever given to a sitting or former member of Congress at the time of sentencing. Bribes included yacht, Rolls-Royce, antique furniture, Persian rugs, and cash. Died August 27, 2025.

name: Jesse Jackson Jr.
charges: Conspiracy to commit wire fraud, mail fraud, and false statements (1 count) — misuse of $750,000 in campaign funds for personal expenditures
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, District of Columbia (Federal)
outcome: Pleaded guilty February 20, 2013. Sentenced to 30 months (2.5 years) in federal prison. Wife Sandra Jackson sentenced separately to 12 months for filing false tax returns.
source1_description: DOJ USAO-DC - Former Congressman Jesse L. Jackson Jr. Pleads Guilty
source2_description: NPR - Guilty Plea From Jesse Jackson Jr. Over 'Lavish' Spending
notes: Used campaign funds for Rolex watch ($43,000), fur capes and parkas, Michael Jackson memorabilia, electronics, and home renovations. Resigned from Congress November 2012.

name: Dennis Hastert
role_title: Speaker of the U.S. House of Representatives; U.S. Representative
charges: Illegal structuring of cash bank withdrawals to evade reporting requirements (1 felony count) — related to paying hush money to cover up decades of child sexual abuse
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Northern District of Illinois (Federal)
outcome: Pleaded guilty October 28, 2015. Sentenced to 15 months in federal prison and $250,000 fine on April 27, 2016. Also required to register as sex offender.
source1_description: DOJ USAO-NDIL - Statement Following Guilty Plea of Former Speaker Hastert
source2_description: NPR - Former House Speaker Dennis Hastert Sentenced to 15 Months in Prison
notes: Longest-serving Republican Speaker of the House. Convicted charge was bank fraud/structuring; sentencing judge called him a 'serial child molester' based on statements submitted to court. Was second in line to the presidency at height of career.

name: Bob Menendez
charges: Conspiracy to commit bribery, honest services wire fraud, extortion under color of official right, obstruction of justice (multiple counts); conspiracy for a public official to act as a foreign agent; acting as foreign agent (total 16+ counts)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York (Federal)
outcome: Convicted by jury on all charges on July 16, 2024. Sentenced to 11 years in federal prison and $922,188.10 in forfeiture on January 29, 2025. Resigned from Senate August 2024. Start of sentence delayed to June 2025.
source1_description: DOJ USAO-SDNY - Former U.S. Senator Robert Menendez Sentenced To 11 Years
source2_description: Bob Menendez Wikipedia - Trial, conviction, and sentencing
notes: Three-term senator from New Jersey; bribery scheme involved gold bars and cash bribes totaling hundreds of thousands of dollars. Also convicted of acting as an illegal foreign agent for Egypt and Qatar. Wife Nadine Menendez convicted separately in April 2025. Menendez has vowed to appeal.

name: George Santos
charges: Wire fraud and aggravated identity theft — defrauded campaign donors and stole identities to fund congressional campaign
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Eastern District of New York (Federal)
outcome: Pleaded guilty in August 2024. Sentenced to 87 months (7 years, 3 months) in federal prison on April 25, 2025. Ordered to pay $373,749.97 in restitution and $205,002.97 in forfeiture. Began serving sentence July 25, 2025. Previously expelled from Congress December 2023.
source1_description: DOJ USAO-EDNY - Ex-Congressman George Santos Sentenced to 87 Months in Prison
source2_description: PBS NewsHour - Disgraced former Rep. Santos to begin serving 7-year fraud sentence
notes: Santos fabricated nearly his entire biographical background including education, employment, and heritage. Expelled from House December 2023 — only sixth member ever expelled. First member of Congress imprisoned for aggravated identity theft.

name: Jim Traficant
charges: Bribery, racketeering, and tax evasion (10 felony counts) — ran congressional office as racketeering enterprise; required staff to pay kickbacks from federal salaries and do chores at personal farm
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Northern District of Ohio (Federal)
outcome: Convicted by jury on all 10 counts on April 11, 2002. Sentenced to 8 years in federal prison and $150,000 fine on July 30, 2002. Expelled from House by 420-1 vote. Served 7 years; released in 2009.
source1_description: PBS NewsHour - Former Ohio Congressman Sentenced to Eight Years in Prison
source2_description: NYT - Ohio Congressman Guilty In Bribery and Kickbacks
notes: Represented himself at trial. Expelled from House in only the second such expulsion in modern era. Previously served as Mahoning County Sheriff. Died in a farm equipment accident in 2014.

name: William J. Jefferson
charges: Bribery, racketeering, money laundering, obstruction of justice, and related charges (11 of 16 counts)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Eastern District of Virginia (Federal)
outcome: Convicted by jury on 11 of 16 counts on August 5, 2009. Sentenced to 13 years in federal prison on November 13, 2009 — the longest sentence ever given to a representative for bribery.
source1_description: William J. Jefferson corruption case - Wikipedia
source2_description: NPR - William 'Cold Cash' Jefferson Convicted Of Corruption
notes: Famous for FBI finding $90,000 in marked bribe money hidden in his home freezer in 2005. FBI also videotaped him accepting $100,000 cash in a briefcase. Accepted more than $400,000 in bribes for brokering business deals in Africa.

name: Chaka Fattah
charges: Racketeering conspiracy, bribery, bank fraud, mail fraud, money laundering, making false statements, and falsification of records (23 counts; 16 counts after partial appeals court reversal)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Eastern District of Pennsylvania (Federal)
outcome: Convicted on 23 counts on June 21, 2016. Initially sentenced to 10 years on December 12, 2016. Appeals court reversed 4 counts in 2018; resentenced to same 10 years on July 12, 2019. Resigned from Congress June 2016.
source1_description: DOJ OPA - Former Congressman Chaka Fattah Sentenced to 10 Years in Prison
source2_description: NPR - Former Rep. Chaka Fattah Sentenced To 10 Years For 'White-Collar Crime Spree'
notes: Pennsylvania congressman misused hundreds of thousands of taxpayer dollars, charitable donations, and campaign funds. Accepted $18,000 bribe from man seeking ambassadorship. Scheduled for release in 2025.

name: Corrine Brown
charges: Wire and mail fraud conspiracy, concealment of material facts on financial disclosures, obstruction of IRS, filing false tax returns (18 of 22 counts)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Middle District of Florida (Federal)
outcome: Convicted by jury on 18 counts on May 11, 2017. Sentenced to 5 years in federal prison on December 4, 2017. Released in 2020 on humanitarian grounds due to COVID-19 after serving ~2 years, 8 months.
source1_description: DOJ OPA - Former U.S. Congresswoman Corrine Brown Sentenced to Prison for Fraud
source2_description: Corrine Brown Wikipedia - Conviction and sentencing
notes: Ran sham nonprofit 'One Door For Education' that collected over $800,000 in donations but gave only $1,200 in scholarships; remaining funds used for personal and political expenses. 12-term Florida congresswoman.

name: Anthony Weiner
charges: Transferring obscene material to a minor (1 count) — sending sexually explicit messages and images to a 15-year-old girl
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York (Federal)
outcome: Pleaded guilty May 19, 2017. Sentenced to 21 months in federal prison on September 25, 2017. Also required to register as a sex offender. Served 18 months (released early for good behavior); began sentence November 2017, released May 2019.
source1_description: DOJ USAO-SDNY - Anthony Weiner Sentenced to 21 Months in Prison
source2_description: NYT - Anthony Weiner Released From Prison After Serving 18 Months
notes: Former New York City mayoral candidate. Had resigned from Congress in 2011 amid earlier sexting scandal. This second scandal, involving a minor, led to criminal charges and federal conviction.

name: Rick Renzi
charges: Extortion, bribery, insurance fraud, money laundering, and racketeering (17 of 32 counts) — used congressional vote to force land deal benefiting former business partner who owed him $700,000
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, District of Arizona (Federal)
outcome: Convicted by jury on 17 counts on June 11, 2013. Sentenced to 36 months in federal prison and fine on October 28, 2013. Released from prison in 2017. Pardoned by President Trump on January 20, 2021.
source1_description: DOJ OPA - Former Congressman Richard G. Renzi Sentenced for Extortion and Bribery
source2_description: Cronkite News - Trump pardons former Rep. Rick Renzi for fraud, extortion convictions
notes: Used money looted from clients of his insurance agency to fund his 2002 campaign. Forced copper mine owners to buy land from former business partner as condition of congressional support.

name: Michael Flynn
government_body: Executive Office of the President (Trump White House)
charges: Making false statements to FBI agents (1 count) — lied about conversations with Russian Ambassador Sergey Kislyak regarding sanctions
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, District of Columbia (Federal)
outcome: Pleaded guilty December 1, 2017. Never sentenced — Trump granted a full pardon on November 25, 2020, ending proceedings. Conviction stands in record prior to pardon.
source1_description: White House - Statement on Executive Grant of Clemency to General Michael T. Flynn
source2_description: AP News - Trump pardons Flynn despite guilty plea in Russia probe
notes: Only White House official convicted during the Mueller investigation. Had previously served as Director of the Defense Intelligence Agency (Lieutenant General). DOJ had moved to drop charges under AG Barr before pardon was issued. Also admitted in plea to failing to register as a foreign agent for Turkey.

name: Elliott Abrams
role_title: Assistant Secretary of State for Inter-American Affairs
charges: Unlawfully withholding information from Congress (2 misdemeanor counts) — gave false testimony about U.S. involvement in arming Nicaraguan Contras during Iran-Contra affair
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, District of Columbia (Federal)
outcome: Pleaded guilty on 2 misdemeanor counts in 1991. Sentenced to $50 fine, 2 years probation, and 100 hours community service. Pardoned by President George H.W. Bush on December 24, 1992.
source1_description: Politico - Bush pardons Iran-Contra felons, Dec. 24, 1992
source2_description: Elliott Abrams Wikipedia - Iran-Contra affair and convictions
notes: One of several Iran-Contra figures convicted and pardoned by Bush. Later publicly sanctioned by D.C. Bar in 1997 for false congressional testimony. Subsequently served as Special Envoy for Venezuela (2019) and other senior roles under subsequent administrations.

name: I. Lewis 'Scooter' Libby
role_title: Chief of Staff to Vice President Dick Cheney
government_body: Office of the Vice President (Bush Administration)
charges: Perjury (2 counts), obstruction of justice (1 count), and making false statements (1 count) — lied to FBI and grand jury about role in leaking CIA officer Valerie Plame's identity
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, District of Columbia (Federal)
outcome: Convicted by jury on 4 of 5 counts on March 6, 2007. Sentenced to 30 months in prison, 2 years probation, and $250,000 fine. Prison sentence commuted by President Bush on July 2, 2007 (never served prison time). Fully pardoned by President Trump on April 13, 2018.
source1_description: White House Archives - Statement by President Bush on Executive Clemency for Lewis Libby
source2_description: NPR - On This Day In 2007, Bush Commutes Scooter Libby Sentence
notes: Central figure in 'Plame Affair.' Convicted on obstruction charges but Bush called prison sentence 'excessive' and commuted it without pardoning. Conviction stood for over a decade until Trump pardon in 2018.

name: Peter Navarro
role_title: Director of the Office of Trade and Manufacturing Policy; White House Trade Adviser
government_body: Executive Office of the President (Trump White House)
charges: Contempt of Congress (2 counts) — refused to comply with subpoena from House Select Committee investigating January 6 Capitol attack (failure to appear for deposition and failure to produce documents)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, District of Columbia (Federal)
outcome: Convicted by jury on both counts on September 7, 2023. Sentenced to 4 months in federal prison and $9,500 fine on January 25, 2024. Reported to prison March 19, 2024; released after serving sentence.
source1_description: DOJ USAO-DC - Ex-White House Trade Advisor Peter Navarro Sentenced to Four Months in Prison
source2_description: CNN - Peter Navarro begins serving prison sentence after historic contempt conviction
notes: First former White House official imprisoned due to a contempt of Congress verdict. Argued Trump had claimed executive privilege over his testimony but courts rejected the argument. Conviction remains; Trump did not pardon him during first term.

name: Steve Bannon
role_title: White House Chief Strategist and Counselor
government_body: Executive Office of the President (Trump White House)
charges: Contempt of Congress (2 counts) — refused to comply with House Select Committee subpoena investigating January 6 Capitol attack (refusal to appear for deposition and failure to produce documents)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, District of Columbia (Federal)
outcome: Convicted by jury on both counts on July 22, 2022. Sentenced to 4 months in prison and $6,500 fine on October 21, 2022 (sentence stayed pending appeal). Reported to prison July 1, 2024 after exhausting appeals; released October 29, 2024.
source1_description: DOJ USAO-DC - Stephen K. Bannon Sentenced to Four Months in Prison on Two Counts of Contempt of Congress
source2_description: PBS - Steve Bannon released from prison after serving 4 months for contempt of Congress
notes: Conviction affirmed by D.C. Circuit Court of Appeals (May 2024). Supreme Court declined to intervene. Conviction stands; Trump pardoned him in January 2025 shortly after taking office for second term.

name: Derek Chauvin
charges: State: Unintentional second-degree murder, third-degree murder, second-degree manslaughter. Federal: Deprivation of rights under color of law (civil rights violations) — kneeled on George Floyd's neck for ~9 minutes causing death; also violated civil rights of a 14-year-old in a 2017 incident
jurisdiction: State: Hennepin County District Court, Minnesota. Federal: U.S. District Court, District of Minnesota
outcome: State: Convicted on all 3 counts April 20, 2021; sentenced to 22.5 years in prison on June 25, 2021. Federal: Pleaded guilty December 15, 2021; sentenced to 252 months (21 years) on July 7, 2022, to run concurrently with state sentence.
source1_description: DOJ OPA - Former Minneapolis Police Officer Derek Chauvin Sentenced to More Than 20 Years for Depriving George Floyd of His Constitutional Rights
source2_description: NYT - Derek Chauvin Sentenced to 21 Years for Civil Rights Violations
notes: First white Minnesota police officer convicted of murdering a Black person. Floyd's death on May 25, 2020 sparked global protests. State conviction upheld by Minnesota Supreme Court; U.S. Supreme Court declined review. Currently serving concurrent state and federal sentences.

name: Tou Thao
charges: Federal: Deprivation of rights under color of law (willful failure to intervene in Chauvin's use of unreasonable force on George Floyd; failure to render medical aid). State: Aiding and abetting second-degree manslaughter
jurisdiction: Federal: U.S. District Court, District of Minnesota. State: Hennepin County District Court, Minnesota
outcome: Federal: Convicted by jury February 24, 2022; sentenced to 42 months (3.5 years) in prison on July 27, 2022. State: Convicted of aiding and abetting second-degree manslaughter (bench trial) May 2, 2023; sentenced to 57 months on August 7, 2023. Sentences run concurrently. Released from federal prison November 2025.
source1_description: DOJ OPA - Former Minneapolis Police Officers Tou Thao and J. Alexander Kueng Sentenced to Prison
source2_description: Minnesota Attorney General - May 2, 2023 Press Release on Thao State Conviction
notes: Stood by watching as Chauvin knelt on Floyd's neck without intervening. Federal conviction for failure to intervene and failure to provide medical aid. Also convicted in state court in separate bench trial. Released from federal custody November 2025.

name: J. Alexander Kueng
charges: Federal: Deprivation of rights under color of law (failure to intervene in Chauvin's excessive force and failure to render medical aid to George Floyd). State: Aiding and abetting second-degree manslaughter (guilty plea)
jurisdiction: Federal: U.S. District Court, District of Minnesota. State: Hennepin County District Court, Minnesota
outcome: Federal: Convicted by jury February 24, 2022; sentenced to 36 months (3 years) in prison on July 27, 2022. State: Pleaded guilty October 2022; sentenced to 42 months (3.5 years) on December 9, 2022. Sentences run concurrently.
source1_description: DOJ OPA - Three Former Minneapolis Police Officers Convicted of Federal Civil Rights Violations
source2_description: 6ABC - Officer who knelt on George Floyd's back sentenced to 3.5 years
notes: Knelt on Floyd's back during the arrest; failed to intervene in Chauvin's excessive force and failed to provide medical aid. One of only three officers in U.S. history convicted federally for depriving someone of civil rights by failure to intervene.

name: Thomas Lane
charges: Federal: Deprivation of rights under color of law (failure to render medical aid to George Floyd). State: Aiding and abetting second-degree manslaughter (guilty plea)
jurisdiction: Federal: U.S. District Court, District of Minnesota. State: Hennepin County District Court, Minnesota
outcome: Federal: Convicted by jury February 24, 2022; sentenced to 30 months (2.5 years) in prison on July 21, 2022. State: Pleaded guilty May 2022; sentenced to 3 years on September 21, 2022 (to run concurrently with federal). Released from federal prison August 2024.
source1_description: DOJ OPA - Former Minneapolis Police Officer Thomas Lane Sentenced to 30 Months in Prison
source2_description: ABC News - Thomas Lane, former cop convicted in George Floyd's death, released from prison
notes: Held Floyd's legs during the fatal arrest. Failure to provide medical aid and failure to intervene. Released from federal custody August 2024 after completing federal sentence.

name: Lee Baca
charges: Conspiracy to obstruct justice, obstruction of justice, and making false statements to federal investigators (3 felony counts) — led scheme to obstruct FBI investigation into corruption and civil rights abuses in county jails
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Central District of California (Federal)
outcome: Convicted by jury on all 3 felony counts on March 15, 2017. Sentenced to 3 years in federal prison on May 12, 2017. Reported to prison February 5, 2020 (after years of appeals). Released January 14, 2022.
source1_description: DOJ USAO-CDCA - Former L.A. County Sheriff Lee Baca Sentenced to 3 Years in Federal Prison
source2_description: DOJ USAO-CDCA - Former L.A. County Sheriff Lee Baca Found Guilty
notes: Sheriff from 1998 to 2014. Led scheme in which deputies smuggled cell phones to an inmate who was an FBI informant, moved the informant between jails, and confronted an FBI agent. 10 subordinates also convicted in related cases.

name: Jason Van Dyke
charges: Second-degree murder (1 count) and aggravated battery with a firearm (16 counts) — shot 17-year-old Laquan McDonald 16 times in October 2014
jurisdiction: Cook County Circuit Court, Illinois (State)
outcome: Convicted by jury on second-degree murder and 16 counts of aggravated battery on October 5, 2018. Sentenced to 81 months (6 years, 9 months) in prison on January 18, 2019. Released early February 2022 for good behavior — served less than half of sentence.
source1_description: Murder of Laquan McDonald Wikipedia - conviction details and case number
source2_description: Injustice Watch - Data Shows Leniency of Ex-Officer Van Dyke's Murder Sentence
notes: First Chicago officer convicted of murder for on-duty shooting in over 50 years. Dashcam footage showed McDonald walking away when shot; city withheld footage for over a year. Sentence was controversially lenient — average sentence for same charge was 12 years. Released February 2022.

name: Daniel Holtzclaw
charges: First-degree rape, sexual battery, indecent exposure, stalking, forcible oral sodomy, burglary, second-degree rape (18 of 36 counts) — sexually assaulted multiple Black women while on duty by exploiting their criminal records as leverage
jurisdiction: Oklahoma County District Court, Oklahoma (State)
outcome: Convicted by jury on 18 of 36 charges on December 10, 2015. Sentenced to 263 years in prison on January 21, 2016 — effectively life in prison. Oklahoma Court of Criminal Appeals upheld convictions and sentence on August 1, 2019.
source1_description: Oklahoma Court of Criminal Appeals - HOLTZCLAW v. STATE opinion (conviction upheld 2019)
source2_description: Daniel Holtzclaw Wikipedia - conviction and sentencing
notes: Targeted victims (8 women, ages 17-57) who had outstanding warrants or criminal records, believing they would not be believed or would fear reporting. Ran background checks before assaults. Conviction and 263-year sentence upheld on appeal.

name: Mohamed Noor
charges: Third-degree murder (later overturned) and second-degree manslaughter — shot and killed unarmed Justine Ruszczyk Damond after she called 911 to report a possible rape behind her home
jurisdiction: Hennepin County District Court, Minnesota (State)
outcome: Convicted by jury of third-degree murder and second-degree manslaughter on April 30, 2019. Initially sentenced to 12.5 years. Murder conviction overturned by Minnesota Supreme Court September 2021; resentenced to 57 months (4 years, 9 months) for manslaughter only on October 21, 2021.
conviction_date: April 30, 2019 (murder later overturned; manslaughter conviction stands)
source1_description: Minnesota Judicial Branch - State of Minnesota v. Mohamed Noor case page
source2_description: CBS Minnesota - Mohamed Noor Resentenced to 57 Months For Manslaughter
notes: Damond was a dual U.S.-Australian citizen from Sydney. Second officer in Minnesota history convicted of murder. Murder (third-degree) conviction vacated by Minnesota Supreme Court in September 2021 as legally inconsistent with existing law; manslaughter conviction stands. Completed sentence June 2022.

name: Brett Hankison
charges: Deprivation of rights under color of law (1 count, federal civil rights) — used excessive and deadly force by firing shots into Breonna Taylor's apartment during a no-knock raid on March 13, 2020
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Western District of Kentucky (Federal)
outcome: Convicted by jury on 1 federal civil rights count on November 1, 2024. Sentenced to 33 months (2 years, 9 months) in federal prison on July 21, 2025, plus 3 years supervised release.
source1_description: DOJ OPA - Former Louisville, Kentucky Metro Police Officer Found Guilty of Federal Civil Rights Crimes
source2_description: NPR - Former officer gets 33 months in prison for violating Breonna Taylor's rights
notes: Hankison fired 10 rounds blindly through a covered patio door and window. Bullets entered an adjacent apartment. Taylor's death sparked major national protests in 2020. Hankison was the only officer directly linked to the raid convicted in connection with Taylor's death.

name: Kelly Goodlett
charges: Conspiracy to violate civil rights (1 count, federal) and conspiracy to obstruct justice — helped falsify the affidavit used to obtain the search warrant for Breonna Taylor's apartment, then conspired to cover it up
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Western District of Kentucky (Federal)
outcome: Pleaded guilty on August 23, 2022. Sentencing pending as of mid-2025 (delayed multiple times). Faces maximum of 5 years in prison.
source1_description: DOJ OPA - Former Louisville, Kentucky Police Detective Pleads Guilty to a Federal Crime Related to Death of Breonna Taylor
source2_description: Courier Journal - Breonna Taylor case: Where cases stand after Brett Hankison sentencing
notes: Goodlett was not present during the raid but was key to obtaining the fraudulent warrant. She also met with co-detective Joshua Jaynes after Taylor's death to get their story straight. Sentencing has been repeatedly delayed; as of mid-2025, awaiting final sentencing.

name: Edwin Edwards
charges: Racketeering (RICO), mail fraud, wire fraud, extortion, money laundering (17 counts)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Middle District of Louisiana (Federal)
outcome: Convicted May 9, 2000 on 17 counts. Sentenced January 8, 2001 to 10 years in federal prison and fined $250,000. Served 8 years; released from federal prison February 2011.
source1_description: DOJ press release — Former Louisiana Governor Edwin Edwards Convicted
source2_description: NPR — Edwin Edwards, Colorful Louisiana Governor Convicted Of Corruption, Dies At 93
notes: Four-term Louisiana governor convicted of extorting hundreds of thousands in bribes from businessmen seeking riverboat casino licenses. Edwards was 72 at time of conviction. Famous for his colorful personality and political comebacks. Died July 12, 2021.

name: Joe Ganim
charges: 16 federal counts: racketeering, racketeering conspiracy, bribery, extortion, mail fraud
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, District of Connecticut (Federal)
outcome: Convicted March 19, 2003 on all 16 counts. Sentenced July 1, 2003 to 9 years in federal prison and ordered to pay $300,000 restitution. Served 7 years; released July 2010. REMARKABLY, was re-elected as Bridgeport mayor in November 2015 and is still serving as of 2025.
source1_description: DOJ press release — Former Bridgeport Mayor Joseph Ganim Convicted of Corruption
source2_description: NPR — Convicted Of Corruption, Bridgeport Mayor Seeks Third Term
notes: Ganim accepted hundreds of thousands in bribes and kickbacks from city contractors while mayor 1991-2003. After serving prison time, he was remarkably RE-ELECTED as Bridgeport mayor in 2015 and has been mayor again since. Unique case of a convicted felon returning to same office.

name: Larry Langford
charges: 60 counts: bribery, conspiracy, fraud, money laundering, filing false tax returns
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Northern District of Alabama (Federal)
outcome: Convicted October 28, 2009 on all 60 counts. Sentenced February 4, 2010 to 15 years in federal prison. Died January 29, 2019 in prison at age 72.
source1_description: DOJ press release — Former Birmingham Mayor Larry Langford Sentenced to 15 Years
source2_description: AL.com — Larry Langford, convicted former Birmingham mayor, dies at 72
notes: Langford accepted over $235,000 in cash, expensive clothes, and jewelry as bribes while serving in county government, then became Birmingham mayor. Convicted on all 60 counts in corruption scheme involving bond deals. Died in federal prison after serving 9 years.

name: Jinchao Wei
government_body: U.S. Navy (assigned to USS Essex, amphibious assault ship)
charges: Conspiracy to send defense information to China; transmitting national defense information to China (2 counts espionage-related charges)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of California (Federal)
outcome: Pleaded guilty January 2024 to conspiracy to send defense information to China and accepting bribes. Sentenced November 2024 to 27 months (2 years 3 months) in federal prison. Navy dismissed him with a dishonorable discharge.
source1_description: DOJ press release — Navy Sailor Sentenced to Over Two Years for Providing Sensitive Military Information to China
source2_description: NPR — Navy sailor gets 2 years for providing sensitive military information to China
notes: Wei, naturalized U.S. citizen from China, transmitted sensitive U.S. military information to Chinese intelligence officer 2022-2023, including warship blueprints and operational plans. Accepted $14,000+ in bribes. Part of two separate Navy espionage cases charged on same day in August 2023.

name: Wenheng Zhao (Thomas Zhao)
government_body: U.S. Navy (assigned to Naval Base Ventura County, California)
charges: Conspiracy to send defense information to China; receiving bribes (2 counts espionage-related charges)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of California (Federal)
outcome: Pleaded guilty October 2023 to conspiracy to send defense information to China. Sentenced November 2024 to 27 months (2 years 3 months) in federal prison and $5,500 fine. Navy dismissed him with a dishonorable discharge.
source1_description: DOJ press release — Navy Sailor Sentenced for Sending Sensitive U.S. Military Information to China
source2_description: Military.com — Navy sailor sentenced to 2 years for sending military information to China
notes: Zhao, naturalized U.S. citizen from China, transmitted sensitive U.S. military photos and information to Chinese intelligence officer 2021-2023, including electrical diagrams, operational plans for exercises in Indo-Pacific. Accepted nearly $15,000 in bribes. Part of two separate Navy espionage cases charged on same day in August 2023.

name: Korbein Schultz
government_body: U.S. Army (assigned to Fort Campbell, Kentucky; held Top Secret/SCI clearance)
charges: Conspiracy to obtain and disclose national defense information; conspiracy to disclose classified information; bribery of a public official (espionage-related charges for transmitting classified intelligence to foreign nationals)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Middle District of Tennessee (Federal)
outcome: Arrested March 2024. Indicted on 6 counts of espionage-related charges. Pleaded guilty June 2024. Sentencing scheduled for 2025; faces up to 65 years in prison (though plea deal likely reduces this significantly).
source1_description: DOJ press release — U.S. Army Intelligence Analyst Charged with Conspiracy to Disclose National Defense Information
source2_description: NPR — Army intelligence analyst pleads guilty to selling military secrets
notes: Schultz conspired with foreign co-conspirators to transmit classified national defense information and sensitive but unclassified information in exchange for payments starting June 2022. Sold classified docs for approximately $42,000. Transmitted intelligence summaries, threat assessments, and information on U.S. military operations and capabilities.

name: Joseph Daniel Schmidt
government_body: U.S. Navy Reserve (attached to U.S. Fleet Cyber Command; held Top Secret/SCI clearance)
charges: 6 counts: attempted espionage, attempted transmission of defense information to a foreign government (China), retention of classified national defense information
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Eastern District of Virginia (Federal)
outcome: Arrested March 2022. Pleaded guilty April 2023 to attempted espionage. Sentenced March 6, 2024 to 179 months (14 years 11 months) in federal prison and $2,000 fine.
source1_description: DOJ press release — Navy Reserve Officer Sentenced to Nearly 15 Years for Attempted Espionage
source2_description: Navy Times — Navy IS pleads guilty to attempted espionage
notes: Schmidt attempted to provide classified national defense information to a representative of a foreign government believed to be China. Held Top Secret/SCI clearance and attempted to sell classified information to what he believed was a Chinese intelligence officer (actually undercover FBI). Retained classified materials at his home without authorization.

name: Ji Chaoqun
role_title: Electrical Engineering Student / Chinese Intelligence Agent
government_body: People's Republic of China intelligence services (operated in U.S. as student while enlisted in U.S. Army Reserves)
charges: Conspiracy to act as an agent of a foreign government (China); acting as an agent of a foreign government without notifying the Attorney General
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Northern District of Illinois (Federal)
outcome: Arrested September 2018. Convicted September 2022 after 7-day trial. Sentenced August 9, 2023 to 8 years in federal prison by Judge Ronald Guzman.
source1_description: DOJ press release — Chinese National and U.S. Army Reservist Sentenced to Eight Years in Prison for Espionage
source2_description: NPR — Army Reservist Sentenced To 8 Years In Prison For Spying For China
notes: Ji was Chinese intelligence operative who enlisted in U.S. Army Reserves in 2016 as part of espionage operation directed by China's Ministry of State Security (MSS). Targeted U.S.-based Chinese nationals for recruitment, including scientists and engineers working in aerospace and defense. Gathered intelligence on eight individuals, including Chinese nationals working on behalf of U.S. defense contractors.

name: John Anthony Walker Jr.
role_title: Chief Warrant Officer and Communications Specialist
government_body: U.S. Navy (retired; created Walker spy ring)
charges: Espionage; conspiracy to commit espionage (led family spy ring for Soviet Union 1968-1985)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Eastern District of Virginia (Federal); U.S. District Court, District of Maryland (Federal)
outcome: Arrested May 20, 1985. Pleaded guilty October 28, 1985 to espionage charges in plea deal (in exchange for lenient sentence for his son). Sentenced to life in prison November 6, 1985. Died in federal prison August 28, 2014 at age 77.
source1_description: FBI — Famous Cases: The John Walker Spy Ring
source2_description: Washington Post — John A. Walker Jr., leader of family spy ring, dies at 77
notes: Walker led one of the most damaging spy rings in U.S. history, selling Navy cryptographic secrets to Soviet Union for 17 years (1968-1985). Recruited his son, brother, and friend into spy ring. Compromised over 1 million classified messages. Secretary of Defense Caspar Weinberger said Walker's espionage enabled Soviets to decipher U.S. Navy communications and 'would have been devastating' in wartime. Died in federal prison 2014.

name: Harold James Nicholson
role_title: CIA Station Chief and Senior Officer
charges: Conspiracy to commit espionage; attempted espionage (spying for Russia while serving as CIA officer)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Eastern District of Virginia (Federal)
outcome: Arrested November 16, 1996. Pleaded guilty March 3, 1997. Sentenced June 5, 1997 to 23 years 7 months in prison. Later convicted AGAIN in 2010 on new charges of conspiracy to act as an agent of a foreign government (continued spying from prison with his son's help). Total combined sentence: 31 years. Released January 2023.
source1_description: FBI — Famous Cases: Harold James Nicholson
source2_description: DOJ press release — Former CIA Officer and His Son Convicted of Conspiracy
notes: Highest-ranking CIA officer ever convicted of espionage. Spied for Russia 1994-1996, compromising identities of CIA trainees and assets. Received $300,000 from Russian SVR. Remarkably, continued spying FROM PRISON 2006-2008, using his son Nathan as courier to Russian agents. Son convicted 2010 and sentenced to 5 years. Nicholson released January 2023 after serving ~27 years.

name: Reality Leigh Winner
role_title: Intelligence Contractor (NSA contractor, former Air Force linguist)
government_body: U.S. Air Force (former); contracted to NSA through Pluribus International Corporation
charges: Unauthorized retention and transmission of national defense information (1 count under Espionage Act — leaked classified NSA document to news outlet)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of Georgia (Federal)
outcome: Arrested June 3, 2017. Pleaded guilty June 26, 2018. Sentenced August 23, 2018 to 63 months (5 years 3 months) in federal prison — longest sentence ever for leaking government information to the media. Released from prison June 2021; completed home confinement November 2021. PARDONED by President Biden on December 23, 2024 (full and unconditional pardon).
source1_description: DOJ press release — Federal Contractor Sentenced to More than Five Years in Prison for Illegally Retaining and Transmitting Classified Materials
source2_description: NPR — Biden pardons ex-intelligence contractor Reality Winner
notes: Winner leaked classified NSA report on Russian interference in 2016 U.S. election to The Intercept in May 2017. Arrested after NSA used document watermarking to trace leak. Received longest sentence ever for media leak under Espionage Act. Pardoned by Biden December 2024, nullifying conviction.

name: John Kiriakou
charges: Unauthorized disclosure of classified information; violating Intelligence Identities Protection Act (leaked identity of covert CIA officer to journalist)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Eastern District of Virginia (Federal)
outcome: Indicted January 2012 on 5 counts. Pleaded guilty October 22, 2012 to one count of disclosing classified information as part of plea deal. Sentenced January 25, 2013 to 30 months (2.5 years) in federal prison. Released February 3, 2015.
source1_description: DOJ press release — Former CIA Officer Sentenced for Disclosing Classified Information
source2_description: NPR — Former CIA Officer Sentenced To Prison For Leaking Operative's Name
notes: Kiriakou was first CIA officer to publicly confirm use of waterboarding on terrorism suspects (2007 interview). Later charged with leaking classified information including identity of covert CIA officer to journalists investigating CIA interrogation program. First CIA officer convicted under Obama administration's crackdown on leaks.

name: Michael Slager
charges: Federal civil rights violation: deprivation of rights under color of law (using excessive force resulting in death of Walter Scott)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, District of South Carolina (Federal)
outcome: State murder trial ended in mistrial December 2016. Pleaded guilty to federal civil rights charge May 2, 2017. Sentenced December 7, 2017 to 20 years in federal prison by Judge David Norton.
source1_description: DOJ press release — Former South Carolina Police Officer Sentenced to 20 Years for Civil Rights Violation
source2_description: NPR — Ex-Officer Michael Slager Gets 20 Years For Walter Scott Killing
notes: Slager shot and killed Walter Scott, an unarmed 50-year-old Black man, on April 4, 2015 after traffic stop. Bystander video showed Slager shooting Scott 5 times in the back as Scott ran away, then appearing to drop his Taser near Scott's body. Video contradicted Slager's initial claim of self-defense. Case became major flashpoint in national debate over police violence.

name: Mark Ciavarella
role_title: Judge, Luzerne County Court of Common Pleas, Pennsylvania
charges: Racketeering, honest services mail fraud, money laundering conspiracy, tax fraud — 'Kids for Cash' scandal; received $2.8 million in kickbacks to sentence juveniles to privately-run detention centers (scheduled release June 2034)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, M.D. Pennsylvania (federal)
outcome: 28 years in federal prison
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Ciavarella sentencing
source2_description: Philadelphia Inquirer Kids for Cash coverage

name: Michael Conahan
role_title: Judge (President Judge), Luzerne County Court of Common Pleas, Pennsylvania
charges: Racketeering conspiracy — 'Kids for Cash' scandal co-conspirator with Ciavarella; accepted $2.8 million in kickbacks from private juvenile detention facility operators
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, M.D. Pennsylvania (federal)
outcome: 17.5 years sentenced; released to home confinement 2020; sentence commuted by President Biden Dec. 11, 2024
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Conahan sentencing
source2_description: White House commutation announcement

name: Vincent 'Buddy' Cianci Jr.
role_title: Mayor of Providence, Rhode Island (two separate stints: 1975–1984 and 1991–2002)
charges: Racketeering conspiracy (Operation Plunder Dome — running a corrupt criminal enterprise using city contracts and jobs for political contributions); earlier 1984 no-contest plea: felony assault (kidnapping and assault of a man) (died Jan. 28, 2016)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, D. Rhode Island (federal)
outcome: 5 years 4 months federal prison, released 2007 (died Jan. 28, 2016)
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Cianci conviction
source2_description: Providence Journal Operation Plunder Dome coverage

name: Catherine Pugh
role_title: Mayor of Baltimore, Maryland (2016–2019); former Maryland State Senator
charges: Conspiracy to commit wire fraud, conspiracy to defraud the United States, tax evasion (2 counts) — 'Healthy Holly' children's book scheme; sold books to politically connected entities without delivering them, double-sold books, used proceeds for straw campaign donations
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, D. Maryland (federal)
outcome: 3 years in federal prison; $411,948 restitution; $669,688 forfeiture
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Pugh sentencing
source2_description: Baltimore Sun Healthy Holly scandal coverage

name: Mel Reynolds
charges: Criminal sexual assault (12 counts), criminal sexual abuse, obstruction of justice, solicitation of child pornography (1995) — sexual relationship with 16-year-old campaign volunteer; bank fraud, wire fraud (16 counts, 1997); failure to file federal income tax returns (4 counts, 2018)
jurisdiction: Illinois state court (1995); U.S. District Court, N.D. Illinois (1997, 2018)
outcome: Multiple — 5 years sexual assault; bank fraud sentence commuted by President Clinton; 6 months for 2018 tax conviction
source1_description: Chicago Tribune Reynolds conviction coverage
source2_description: DOJ Press Release on 2018 tax conviction

name: Dan Rostenkowski
role_title: U.S. Representative (D-IL), 8th Congressional District (18 terms); Chairman, House Ways and Means Committee
charges: Mail fraud (2 counts) — Congressional Post Office Scandal; kept 'ghost' employees on payroll, used Congressional funds for personal gifts, tampered with grand jury witness
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, D. Columbia (federal)
outcome: 17 months; $100,000 fine; pardoned by President Clinton December 2000
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Rostenkowski conviction
source2_description: Chicago Tribune coverage

name: Sheldon Silver
charges: Honest services wire fraud (2 counts), honest services mail fraud (2 counts), extortion under color of official right (2 counts), engaging in illegal monetary transactions — accepted nearly $4 million in bribes in exchange for steering state cancer research grants and personal injury client referrals (died in custody November 2022)
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, S.D. New York (federal)
outcome: 7 years; $1.75 million fine; $4 million forfeiture; died in custody 2022
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Silver conviction
source2_description: New York Times coverage

name: Dean Skelos
role_title: Majority Leader, New York State Senate (2008–2015)
charges: Bribery, extortion, conspiracy — used his Senate position to coerce businesses (real estate developer, environmental technology company, medical malpractice insurer) to provide his son Adam Skelos with approximately $300,000 in no-show or low-show jobs and payments
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, S.D. New York (federal)
outcome: 4 years 3 months; released to house arrest Apr. 2020 due to COVID-19
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Skelos conviction
source2_description: New York Times coverage

name: Larry Householder
charges: Racketeering conspiracy — led a criminal enterprise that accepted nearly $61 million in bribes (funded by FirstEnergy Corp.) to pass and defend House Bill 6, a $1.1 billion nuclear plant bailout, and block a repeal referendum through fraudulent spending
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, S.D. Ohio (federal)
outcome: 20 years in federal prison
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Householder sentencing
source2_description: Columbus Dispatch coverage

name: Gordon Fox
role_title: Speaker, Rhode Island House of Representatives (2010–2014)
charges: Wire fraud, bribery, filing a false tax return — stole $108,000 in campaign contributions for personal expenses; accepted $52,000 in bribes to facilitate a liquor license; failed to report illegal income
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, D. Rhode Island (federal)
outcome: 3 years; $109,000 restitution; released February 2018
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Fox sentencing
source2_description: Providence Journal coverage

name: Don Siegelman
role_title: Governor of Alabama (1999–2003); also served as Alabama Secretary of State, Attorney General, and Lieutenant Governor
charges: Bribery, conspiracy to commit honest services mail fraud, honest services mail fraud (4 counts), obstruction of justice — appointed former HealthSouth CEO Richard Scrushy to a state regulatory board in exchange for $500,000 in campaign contributions
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, M.D. Alabama (federal)
outcome: 78 months / 6.5 years; $50,000 fine; released from prison 2017
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Siegelman conviction
source2_description: Alabama news coverage

name: Bob Ney
charges: Conspiracy to commit multiple offenses (honest services fraud, making false statements, aiding violations of federal lobbying ban) — accepted gifts, meals, golf trips to Scotland, and other benefits from lobbyist Jack Abramoff in exchange for political favors; first congressman indicted in the Abramoff lobbying scandal
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, D. Columbia (federal)
outcome: 30 months; $6,000 fine; released after 17 months
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Ney sentencing
source2_description: Washington Post Abramoff scandal coverage

name: Frank Ballance
role_title: U.S. Representative (D-NC), 1st Congressional District (2003–2004); former North Carolina State Senator
charges: Conspiracy to commit mail fraud and money laundering — funneled $2.3 million in state funds obtained as a state senator through a youth foundation and used the money for personal enrichment of himself, family members, and his church
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, E.D. North Carolina (federal)
outcome: 4 years; $10,000 fine; disbarred January 2005; released June 2009
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Ballance sentencing
source2_description: Raleigh News & Observer coverage

name: John Rowland
role_title: Governor of Connecticut (1995–2004); U.S. Representative (R-CT) (1985–1991) — first Connecticut governor to serve prison time
charges: Conspiracy to commit honest services mail fraud and tax fraud (2004) — accepted illegal gifts worth over $100,000 as governor; falsification of records, causing false FEC statements and illegal campaign contributions (7 counts, 2014) — concealed $35,000 in payments
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, D. Connecticut (federal)
outcome: 12 months 2005; 30 months 2015; pardoned by President Trump May 28, 2025
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Rowland 2005 conviction
source2_description: Hartford Courant coverage

name: Glen Casada
role_title: Speaker, Tennessee House of Representatives (2019); Republican state representative
charges: Conspiracy, honest services wire fraud (multiple counts), money laundering — with former Chief of Staff Cade Cothren, received over $50,000 in taxpayer funds through a fraudulent mailer program for lawmakers; convicted on 14 of 17 counts
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, M.D. Tennessee (federal)
outcome: 3 years / 36 months; pardoned by President Trump November 7, 2025 before reporting to prison
source1_description: DOJ Press Release on Casada sentencing
source2_description: Nashville Tennessean coverage

name: Michael Madigan
role_title: Speaker of the Illinois House of Representatives; Chairman, Illinois Democratic Party
charges: Racketeering conspiracy, bribery, attempted extortion — led scheme to use public office to enrich allies, including accepting bribes from ComEd (Commonwealth Edison) for legislative action
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Northern District of Illinois
outcome: Convicted on all counts by jury; sentenced to 7 years in federal prison, April 2025
source1_description: DOJ NDIL — Michael Madigan convicted of racketeering and bribery
source2_description: AP News — Illinois' most powerful politician convicted of corruption

name: José Huizar
charges: RICO conspiracy; bribery; money laundering; tax evasion — led citywide pay-to-play corruption scheme involving real estate developers seeking city approvals
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Central District of California
outcome: Pleaded guilty to RICO conspiracy; sentenced to 13 years in federal prison, April 2024
source1_description: DOJ CDCA — José Huizar sentenced to 13 years for corruption
source2_description: LA Times — Huizar sentenced to 13 years in wide-ranging corruption case

name: Chris Collins
role_title: U.S. Representative, 27th Congressional District of New York
charges: Securities fraud, wire fraud, making false statements to FBI — used advance knowledge of a failed drug trial to tip off son and others to dump stock in Innate Immunotherapeutics
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of New York
outcome: Pleaded guilty; sentenced to 26 months in federal prison, January 2020. Pardoned by President Trump January 20, 2021.
source1_description: DOJ SDNY — Chris Collins sentenced for insider trading
source2_description: CNN — Chris Collins pardoned by Trump
notes: Pardoned by Trump on January 20, 2021 (last day of first term).

name: Duncan Hunter
role_title: U.S. Representative, 50th Congressional District of California
charges: Conspiracy to misuse campaign funds — spent more than $250,000 in campaign funds on personal expenses including family vacations, dental work, video games, and an extramarital affair
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of California
outcome: Pleaded guilty; sentenced to 11 months in federal prison, March 2020. Pardoned by President Trump January 19, 2021.
source1_description: DOJ SDCA — Duncan Hunter sentenced for misusing campaign funds
source2_description: NBC News — Trump pardons Rep. Duncan Hunter
notes: Pardoned by Trump on January 19, 2021.

name: Steve Stockman
role_title: U.S. Representative, 36th Congressional District of Texas (1995–97, 2013–15)
charges: Mail fraud, wire fraud, money laundering — funneled charitable donations through sham nonprofits for personal use
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Southern District of Texas
outcome: Convicted on all 23 counts; sentenced to 10 years in federal prison, November 2018. Pardoned by President Trump December 23, 2020.
source1_description: DOJ SDTX — Steve Stockman sentenced to 10 years for fraud
source2_description: Reuters — Trump pardons former Texas congressman Steve Stockman
notes: Pardoned by Trump on December 23, 2020.

name: Jeff Fortenberry
role_title: U.S. Representative, 1st Congressional District of Nebraska
charges: Making false statements to federal investigators; scheming to falsify material facts — lied to investigators about receiving illegal foreign campaign contributions from Nigerian billionaire Gilbert Chagoury
jurisdiction: U.S. District Court, Central District of California
outcome: Convicted on all three counts; sentenced to 2 years probation, $25,000 fine, March 2022. Resigned from Congress two days after conviction.
source1_description: DOJ CDCA — Jeff Fortenberry convicted of making false statements
source2_description: AP News — Rep. Fortenberry convicted, resigns from Congress
